learningGoal: How to understand what the % symbol means
Why 50% is the same as half

screens.title: What does 50% actually mean?
screens.body:
Aisha is counting coloured squares on a 100-square grid. Out of **100** squares, **50** are coloured in.

That's **50%** — but where does the word "percent" come from?

screens.title: Per cent = per hundred!
screens.body: The word **percent** comes from Latin: **per centum**, meaning **"out of one hundred"**. Tap each step to see how a percentage becomes a fraction!

interaction.sentence: "Per cent" comes from Latin meaning "out of ____"
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! "Cent" means hundred — like century or centimetre. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — "cent" means hundred, like century!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: If 30 squares out of 100 are coloured, what percentage is that?

interaction.question: 30 out of 100 = what percentage?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 30 out of 100 = **30%**. "Per cent" literally means "out of 100"! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The number out of 100 IS the percentage. 30 out of 100 = **30%**.

learningGoal: How to apply the 'out of 100' idea to real-life situations
When to watch out for confusing a percentage with a fraction

screens.title: What does 80% really mean?
screens.body:
Oliver is scoring on a test with 100 questions. **80** out of **100** — that's **80%**.

But why do we use 100? Let's find out!

screens.title: Key fact: % always means out of 100
screens.body: Using 100 as a common base makes comparing amounts easy. Is 3/7 bigger than 5/12? Hard to tell! But 43% vs 42%? Easy! Tap to see how this works.

steps.text: 100 is a handy base for comparing
steps.why: Everything over the same denominator (bottom number) ✓

screens.title: Quick check!
screens.body: What fraction is the same as **40%**?

interaction.question: 40% is the same as which fraction?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 40% means 40 out of 100 = 40/100 = **2/5**.

learningGoal: How to recall which fractions match the most useful percentages
How to quickly recall 50%, 25%, 10%, 75%, 20%

screens.title: The 50% shortcut!
screens.body:
Lauren is learning quick tricks for her 11+ exam. She needs **50%** of **80**.

Good news: **50%** = **1/2** = half. So just **divide by 2**!

screens.title: The five percentages to memorise
screens.body: You saw that **50%** of **80** = 80 ÷ 2 = **40**. That works because 50% = 1/2. There are **five** key percentages like this — memorise them and you'll be much faster! Tap to reveal each one.

interaction.question: What is 50% of 120?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 50% = 1/2, so divide by 2: 120 ÷ 2 = **60**.

screens.title: Five percentages to know by heart!

learningGoal: How to avoid mixing up common percentage-fraction pairs
When to watch out for confusing 25% with 20% or 75% with 50%

screens.title: Here's the mistake!
screens.body: Jake confused 25% with 10% — he divided by 10 instead of by 4. The most common error is confusing which fraction goes with which percentage. Tap to see where it went wrong!

steps.text: Wrong: Jake divided 80 by 10 instead of 4

screens.title: What's the correct answer?
screens.body: Now you know the mistake, what is **10%** of **60** really?

learningGoal: How to find 10% of any number by dividing by 10
Why 10% is the building block for all other percentages

screens.title: What is 10% of £60?
screens.body:
Mrs Patel works out the 10% service charge on a restaurant bill of **£60**.

Finding **10%** is one of the most useful skills in maths. And it's surprisingly easy!

screens.title: Just divide by 10!
screens.body: Finding 10% is the single most useful percentage trick — and it is surprisingly simple. Tap each step to learn the method!

interaction.sentence: To find 10% of any number, divide by ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 10% = 1/10, so just divide by 10. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — 10% is the same as 1/10, so you divide by 10.

steps.why: Move every digit one place to the right

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
What is **10%** of **£90**?

Remember: just divide by 10!

interaction.question: What is 10% of £90?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! £90 ÷ 10 = **£9**. Easy when you know the trick! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! To find 10%, divide by 10: £90 ÷ 10 = **£9**.

learningGoal: How to apply the divide-by-10 shortcut to real-life percentage problems
When to watch out for dividing by the wrong number to find 10%

screens.title: The one trick that unlocks ALL percentages!
screens.body:
A pizza restaurant adds a 10% tip to every bill automatically: **£80**.

What if we told you there's **one simple trick** that lets you find lots of different percentages? It all starts with 10%...

screens.title: Divide by 10 — that's it!
screens.body: **10%** equals one tenth — and finding a tenth is the easiest division there is. Once you know this trick, you can build up to almost any percentage. Tap to see it in action!

interaction.question: 10% of £110 = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! £110 ÷ 10 = **£11**. You've unlocked the master building block! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Divide by 10: £110 ÷ 10 = **£11**.

learningGoal: How to build lots of different percentages from 10% and 1% blocks
How to find 5% (half of 10%) and 1% (divide by 100)

screens.title: How do you find 35%?
screens.body:
Aisha works out a 35% discount on a coat costing **£200**.

You might not know 35% straight away, but you DO know how to find 10%. Let's build up from there!

screens.title: Build 35% from blocks
screens.body: Any percentage can be built from just three building blocks: **10%**, **5%**, and **1%**. Tap to see how they combine to make 35%.

interaction.question: What is 25% of £300?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 25% = 2 lots of 10% + one 5%. That's 2 × 30 + 15 = **£75**.

screens.title: Building lots of different percentages — the recipe

learningGoal: How to avoid errors when combining 10% and 1% blocks
When to watch out for using the wrong building blocks for a percentage

screens.title: Here's what went wrong!
screens.body: Jake found 1% instead of 10% — he divided by 100 instead of by 10, then multiplied by 3. The building blocks must be found correctly first — one wrong block throws off the entire calculation. Tap to see the right way.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find the building blocks first, then combine. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always start with 10%, then build 5% and 1% from it.

interaction.question: 20% of 200 = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 20% of 200 = **40**. Build from 10%, not from shortcuts that don't work! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 10% = 20. Build up: **40**.

subConcepts.name: Expressing One Number as a Percentage of Another

learningGoal: How to express one number as a percentage of another
Why you divide and then multiply by 100

screens.title: 18 out of 25 — what percentage?
screens.body:
Priya scored 18 out of 25 on her spelling test. That's **18** out of **25**.

But what percentage is that? There's a simple rule to find out!

screens.title: Divide, then multiply by 100
screens.body: There is a simple two-step method for converting (changing) any "X out of Y" into a percentage. Tap to learn it!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Divide part by whole, then multiply by 100. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first divide the part by the whole, then multiply by 100.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
What is **14** out of **20** as a percentage?

Divide 14 by 20, then multiply by 100.

screens.title: Expressing as a percentage — the recipe

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing the numbers the wrong way round
When to watch out for forgetting to multiply by 100 at the end

screens.title: Can you spot Jake's error?
screens.body:
Jake tried to express **15** out of **60** as a percentage.

Jake's working: 60 ÷ 15 = 4, then 4 × 100 = 400%

What went wrong?

screens.title: Here's the correct method!
screens.body: Jake divided the wrong way round (60 ÷ 15 = 4, then 4 × 100 = 400%). The correct method is always **(part / whole) x 100** — and the order matters! Tap to see it done right.

screens.title: Now you try!
screens.body: What is **8** out of **40** as a percentage?

learningGoal: How to calculate a price after a percentage increase
Why you find the percentage first, then add

screens.title: 20% increase on £30
screens.body:
A toy shop increases prices by 20% after Christmas. A board game was **£30**.

What's the new price after a **20% increase**? Let's work it out step by step!

screens.title: Find the %, then add it on!
screens.body:
Percentage increase is a two-step process:

1. **Find the percentage** of the original amount (use the building-block method: 10%, 5%, 1%)
2. **ADD** the percentage to the original — increase means it gets bigger
3. **Check:** your answer should be MORE than you started with

Tap to see each step!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **£50** after a **20% increase**?

learningGoal: How to apply percentage increase to real shopping and money problems
When to watch out for adding the percentage to the wrong amount

screens.title: Up by 30%!
screens.body:
A museum had 500 visitors last month. This month, visitors went up by 30%. How many visitors this month?

A percentage increase means the amount gets **bigger**. But by how much exactly?

screens.title: Find it, then add it!
screens.body: Start by finding **10%** as your building block, then build up to 30%. Once you have the increase, just add it on. Tap to follow along!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Find the percentage, then add it on. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the percentage amount, then add it to the original.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is 400 increased by 20%?

learningGoal: How to calculate a sale price after a percentage discount
Why you find the percentage first, then subtract

screens.title: 20% off £60!
screens.body:
Lauren spots trainers in a sale with 20% off. They were **£60**.

What's the sale price after **20% off**? Let's work it out!

screens.title: Find the %, then take it away!
screens.body:
Percentage decrease works just like increase — but instead of adding, you **subtract**.

1. **Find the percentage** of the original amount (use the building-block method)
2. **SUBTRACT** from the original — decrease means it gets smaller
3. **Check:** your answer should be LESS than you started with

Tap to see each step of the discount!

interaction.sentence: For a percentage decrease, find the percentage then ____ it from the original
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Decrease means subtract — the answer should be less than the original. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — decrease means it gets smaller, so you subtract.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **£90** after **20% off**?

interaction.question: £90 with 20% off = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 20% of £90 = £18. Take it away: £90 - £18 = **£72**. ✓

learningGoal: How to apply percentage discount calculations to real sale prices
When to watch out for subtracting the percentage instead of the amount

screens.title: Down by 15%!
screens.body:
A school had 400 pupils, but numbers dropped by 15%. How many now?

A percentage decrease means the amount gets **smaller**. But how much smaller?

screens.title: Find it, then take it away!
screens.body: Build up to **15%** using the 10% building block, then take it away from the original. Tap to follow the working!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is 300 decreased by 20%?

interaction.question: 300 decreased by 20% = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 20% = 60. Subtract: 300 - 60 = **240**.

learningGoal: How to write a percentage as a fraction over 100
How to simplify the fraction

screens.title: What is 40% as a fraction?
screens.body: Aisha needs to write **40%** as a fraction. Remember: percent means "out of 100" — so that gives us our starting point!

screens.title: Put it over 100, then simplify!
screens.body: Since percent means "out of 100", any percentage instantly becomes a fraction — then you just simplify. Tap to see the two-step process!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Put over 100, find the highest common factor, then simplify. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first write it over 100, then simplify using the highest common factor.

steps.text: Find the highest common factor (the biggest number that divides into both) of 40 and 100

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Write **20%** as a fraction in its simplest form.

interaction.question: 20% as a simplified fraction?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 20% = 20/100. Simplify by dividing both by 20: **1/5**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 20% = 20/100. Divide both by 20 to get **1/5**.

learningGoal: How to apply percentage-to-fraction conversion in exam questions
When to watch out for not simplifying the fraction fully

screens.title: Quick convert: 35% to a fraction!
screens.body:
Oliver needs to convert (change) **35%** to a fraction.

The key fact: **percent means out of 100**, so you already know the bottom number (denominator)!

screens.title: Key fact: just put it over 100!
screens.body:
The percentage becomes the **numerator** (top number) and 100 is the **denominator** (bottom number).
Then simplify by dividing both by the **highest common factor** (the biggest number that goes into both).

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **45%** as a fraction in simplest form?

interaction.question: 45% = which fraction?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 45% = 45/100, simplified to **9/20**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 45% = 45/100. Divide both by 5: **9/20**.

learningGoal: How to convert (change) a percentage to a decimal by dividing by 100

screens.title: Key fact: % to decimal = divide by 100
screens.body: Converting between percentages and decimals is just about moving the decimal point. But be careful — moving it the **wrong number** of places is the most common trap! Tap to see the rule.

interaction.sentence: To convert a percentage to a decimal, divide by ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Divide by 100 — move the decimal point 2 places left. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — "per cent" means "per hundred", so divide by 100.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **45%** as a decimal?

interaction.question: 45% as a decimal = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 45% ÷ 100 = **0.45**. Two places to the left! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Divide by 100: 45 ÷ 100 = **0.45**. Move the decimal point two places left.

learningGoal: How to avoid moving the decimal point the wrong number of places
When to watch out for the 5% = 0.5 trap

screens.title: Here's the mistake!
screens.body: Jake only moved the decimal one place instead of two — he divided by 10 instead of 100. Remember: converting to a decimal means dividing by **100**, not 10 — that is **two** places, not one! Tap to compare.

steps.why: Jake only moved the decimal one place instead of two — he divided by 10 instead of 100

screens.title: What's the correct decimal?
screens.body: What is **3%** as a decimal?

interaction.question: 3% = which decimal?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 3% ÷ 100 = **0.03**. Two places left! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 3 ÷ 100 = **0.03**. Remember: TWO places to the left.

learningGoal: How to compare a fraction, a decimal and a percentage by converting (changing) to the same type
How to decide which form is easiest to change to

screens.title: Which is biggest?
screens.body:
Aisha is comparing test scores: she got 3/5, Ben got 0.55, and Chloe got 65%.

A fraction, a decimal and a percentage — how do you compare them? **Convert them all to the same type!**

screens.title: Convert everything to percentages!
screens.body:
The easiest way to compare is to convert everything to **percentages**:

- **3/5** = **60%**
- **0.55** = **55%**
- **65%** = **65%**

Now it's easy to compare!

steps.why: Fraction: divide top by bottom, then × 100

screens.title: Which is the largest?
screens.body: 1/2, 0.45, and 55% — convert them all to percentages. Which is the **largest**?

interaction.question: Which is the largest: 1/2, 0.45, or 55%?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Converting to percentages: 50%, 45%, 55%. The largest is **55%** (55%). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Convert all to %: 1/2=50%, 0.45=45%, 55%=55%. Largest is **55%**.

learningGoal: How to apply fraction-decimal-percentage conversion to comparison questions
When to watch out for comparing values that are in different forms

screens.title: Three types, one comparison!
screens.body:
Ben is choosing the best deal on chocolate: bar A is 1/2 cocoa, bar B is 0.45 cocoa, bar C is 55% cocoa.

They're all written differently — but which is the **smallest**?

screens.title: Convert to see the truth!
screens.body:
Look at the bar model — you can already see which is smallest! But to prove it, convert:

- 1/2 = **50%**
- 0.45 = **45%**
- 55% = **55%**

The smallest is **0.45** (45%).

screens.title: Spot the smallest!
screens.body: Which is the **smallest**: 1/4, 0.3, or 20%?

interaction.question: Which is smallest: 1/4, 0.3, or 20%?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Converting to %: 25%, 30%, 20%. Smallest is **20%** (20%). ✓

learningGoal: How to find the original price when you know the discounted price
Why you work out what percentage the given amount represents

screens.title: What was the original price?
screens.body:
Lauren bought a coat in a 20% off sale for £**48**.

The sale was **20% off**. What was the **original** price before the sale?

This is a **reverse percentage** question — we work backwards!

screens.title: Work out what % you have, then scale to 100%
screens.body:
If there's **20% off**, the sale price is **80%** of the original.

So £48 = 80%.

Find 1%: £48 ÷ 80 = £0.6
Find 100%: £0.6 × 100 = **£60**

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
After **20% off**, the price is **£56**. What was the original price?

Remember: £56 = 80%. Find 1%, then 100%.

interaction.question: Original price before 20% off?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! £56 is 80% of the original. £56 ÷ 80 × 100 = **£70**.

learningGoal: How to avoid taking a percentage of the discounted price instead of the original
When to watch out for the reverse percentage trap in exam questions

screens.title: Jake's reverse percentage trap!
screens.body:
Jake paid **£240** after **20% off**. He worked out the original price like this:

£240 + 20% of £240 = £240 + £48 = £288

But something's wrong...

screens.title: The classic reverse percentage trap!
screens.body:
Jake found 20% of £240 (£48) and added it back on — but £240 is NOT 100%, it's 80%!

The correct way: £240 = **80%**.

1% = £240 ÷ 80 = £3
100% = £3 × 100 = **£300**

screens.title: What's the correct original?
screens.body: After **20% off**, the price is **£320**. What was the original price?

interaction.question: Original price before 20% off?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! £320 = 80%, so original = **£400**. Don't fall for the trap! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! £320 is 80% of the original. £320 ÷ 80 × 100 = **£400**.

screens.title: Don't use the sale price as 100%!

learningGoal: Why two 10% discounts don't equal a 20% discount
How to apply percentage changes one step at a time

screens.title: 10% off + 10% off = 20% off... right?
screens.body:
A clothes shop offers 10% off, then another 10% off the sale price. A jacket is **£100**.

You might think 10% off then 10% off is the same as **20% off**. But it's NOT! Let's see why...

screens.title: Apply each discount one at a time!
screens.body:
**Discount 1:** 10% off £100 = £10 off → £90

**Discount 2:** 10% off £90 (NOT £100!) = £9 off → £81

The second discount applies to the **already reduced price**, so you save less than you'd think!

screens.title: What's the final price?
screens.body: **£200** with **20% off**, then **10% off** the sale price. What do you actually pay?

interaction.question: £200 after 20% off then 10% off?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 20% off = £160. Then 10% off that = **£144**. NOT the same as 30% off! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! First: 20% off £200 = £160. Then: 10% off £160 = **£144**.

learningGoal: How to avoid the trap of adding successive percentages together
When to watch out for applying the second percentage to the wrong amount

screens.title: Jake just added the percentages!
screens.body:
A shop gives **10% off**, then another **10% off** the sale price. The item costs **£200**.

Jake's working: 10% + 10% = 20%, so 20% of £200 = £40 off, final = £160

Is that right?

screens.title: You can't just add the percentages!
screens.body:
Jake added 10% + 10% = 20% and took 20% off £200 in one go. But the second discount applies to the **reduced price**, not the original!

**Step 1:** 10% off £200 = £180
**Step 2:** 10% off £180 = £162

screens.title: What's the correct final price?
screens.body: £300 with 10% off, then 10% off the sale price. What do you pay?

interaction.question: £300 after 10% off then 10% off?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 10% off = £270, then 10% off that = **£243**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Step 1: £270. Step 2: 10% off £270 = **£243**.

learningGoal: How to spot whether a problem is asking for increase, decrease, or a simple percentage
How to check your answer makes sense in context

screens.title: What type of percentage question is this?
screens.body:
A clothes shop has a sale with 30% off everything. Aisha buys a dress that was £60 and a scarf that was £20. How much does she spend in total?

Before you start calculating, ask: **what kind of percentage problem is this?** Is it finding a %, increase, decrease, or comparing?

steps.why: Read the question carefully to identify the type

screens.title: Solve it step by step!
screens.body:
This is a **percentage decrease** problem.

Total before sale: £60 + £20 = £80
30% of £80 = £24
Sale price: £80 - £24 = **£56**

screens.title: What's the answer?
screens.body: A different shop has 20% off everything. You buy items totalling £100. What do you pay?

interaction.question: Total sale spend after 20% off?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Total = £100. 20% off = £20. Answer: **£80**.

learningGoal: How to avoid confusing increase, decrease, and simple percentage questions
When to watch out for answers that are bigger or smaller than they should be

screens.title: Did Jake answer the question?
screens.body:
A shop has 20% off. Jake buys a £45 jumper.

Jake's working: 20% of £45 = £9. Answer: £9

Jake did some correct maths, but didn't actually answer the question! Can you see why?

screens.title: Read the question again!
screens.body:
Jake found 20% correctly (£9) but forgot to subtract it from £45 — he gave the discount amount, not the sale price.

The key: always re-read the question at the end. **What is it actually asking for?** The discount or the price? The increase or the new total? A raw score or a percentage?

steps.why: Jake found 20% correctly (£9) but forgot to subtract it from £45 — he gave the discount amount, not the sale price

steps.result: Always check you've answered what was asked ✓

screens.title: What's the actual answer?
screens.body: A shop has 30% off. You buy an item costing £70. What do you pay?

interaction.question: What is the correct final answer?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! The answer is **49**. Always finish the question — don't stop halfway! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The final answer is **49**. Remember to complete all the steps.
